coredata.dc:title: One-pot facile synthesis of green-emitting fluorescent silicon quantum dots for the highly selective and sensitive detection of nitrite in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Na
Wei, Meng-Xia
Huang, Bo-Hui
Guo, Xiao-Feng
Wang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the outstanding properties, fluorescent silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) have attracted immense attention in recent years. In this work, the facile synthesized, environment friendly green-emitting SiQDs (g-SiQDs) were prepared for the detection of nitrite (NO2 −). It has excellent merits including excellent water-dispersible, photo-stability and salt tolerance. The fluorescence peak at 503 nm could be selectively quenched by NO2 − due to the synergy of inner filter effect (IFE) and photo induced electron transfer (PET). There was a linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentration of NO2 − from 0.1 to 6.0 μM (R2 = 0.9969) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 25 nM, and the sensor displayed a significant selectivity toward NO2 −. Furthermore, the g-SiQDs were successfully applied to analysis NO2 − in food samples after simple pretreatment with satisfactory recoveries, and our method has good potential for routine analyze of NO2 − in food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Nitrite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the g-SiQDs
Selectivity of the proposed method for NO2 −

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the g-SiQDs (Inset: the HRTEM image); (b) Particle size distribution histogram of g-SiQDs; (c) XRD patterns of g-SiQDs; (d) FT-IR spectra of g-SiQDs and m-PD.
XPS spectra of the g-SiQDs. (a) Full-scan analysis; (b) C 1s; (c) N 1s; (d) O 1s; (e) Si 2p, respectively.
(a) Excitation and fluorescence spectra of the g-SiQDs (λex/λem = 387/503 nm); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of g-SiQDs (0.1 mg/mL) under different excitation wavelengths (360–410 nm).

Fluorescence intensity of the g-SiQDs (0.1 mg/mL) as a function of (a) pH; (b) fluorescent radiation time; (c) temperature; (d) concentrations of NaCl. λex = 387 nm, error bars refer the standard deviation, n = 3.

Effect of (a), (b) pH (pH 1.0–2.0, HCl–KCl solution; pH 2.5–3.5, Na2HPO4-citrate solution); (c) reaction time; (d) reaction temperature on fluorescence quenching efficiency. λex = 387 nm, error bars refer the standard deviation, n = 3.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the g-SiQDs (0.1 mg/mL) upon addition of various concentrations of NO2 − (from top to bottom, 0 and 20 μM NO2 −, inset shows the fluorescence quenching efficiency as a function of NO2 − concentration); (b) the fluorescence quenching efficiency as a function of NO2 − concentration of 0.1–6.0 μM. λex = 387 nm, error bars refer the standard deviation, n = 3.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the NO2 − (blue line) and fluorescence spectrum of g-SiQDs; (b) FT-IR spectrum of before (red line) and after (black line) reacting with NO2 −; (c) TEM image after reaction (inset: size distributions of the reacted g-SiQDs, calculated with 170 particles); (d) Zeta potential; (e) Influence of NO2 − concentrations on the fluorescence intensity ratio; (f) Fluorescence lifetime curve. Error bars refer the standard deviation, n = 3. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence intensity response of the g-SiQDs to cations (Fe3+
a : 500 μM Fe3+; Fe3+
b : 100 μM Fe3+; others were 500 μM); Effect of anions (500 μM) (b) and some molecules (500 μM) and NO2 − (5 μM) (c) on fluorescence intensity of the g-SiQDs. (d) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of cations, anions and substances in biological or foods (500 μM). λex = 387 nm, error bars refer the standard deviation, n = 3.
Comparison of the different approach with other reported methods for the detection of NO2 −.
Detection of NO2 − in food samples.

head.ce:title: One-pot facile synthesis of green-emitting fluorescent silicon quantum dots for the highly selective and sensitive detection of nitrite in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to the outstanding properties, fluorescent silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) have attracted immense attention in recent years. In this work, the facile synthesized, environment friendly green-emitting SiQDs (g-SiQDs) were prepared for the detection of nitrite (NO2 −). It has excellent merits including excellent water-dispersible, photo-stability and salt tolerance. The fluorescence peak at 503 nm could be selectively quenched by NO2 − due to the synergy of inner filter effect (IFE) and photo induced electron transfer (PET). There was a linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentration of NO2 − from 0.1 to 6.0 μM (R2 = 0.9969) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 25 nM, and the sensor displayed a significant selectivity toward NO2 −. Furthermore, the g-SiQDs were successfully applied to analysis NO2 − in food samples after simple pretreatment with satisfactory recoveries, and our method has good potential for routine analyze of NO2 − in food samples.

Synthesis of green-emitting silicon quantum dots with N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO) and m-phenylenediamine (m-PD), the green fluorescence of the silicon quantum dots can be selectively quenched by NO2 −.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decades, silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) have been comprehensively developed for the fabrication of high performance sensors owing to their aqueous solubility, unique optical properties, good surface modifiability [1] as well as high biocompatibility [2]. Compared to heavy metal semiconductor quantum dots such as CdTe and CdS, metal nanoclusters like copper nanoclusters and metal organic framework materials, SiQDs are environment friendly, non-toxic and can be metabolized to silicic acid, which is easily excreted in the urine [3], while heavy metals can cause certain damage or even poisoning when entered the organism and accumulate to a certain extent. Moreover, SiQDs have much better water solubility [4] than carbon materials [5] and high photo stability, thus SiQDs can be applied to living cell imaging over a long period of time, overcoming the defect of photobleaching of organic fluorophores [6] and toxicity of organic solvents. Consequently, silicon quantum dots have been gradually used for the fluorescence detection of some analytical objects, such as metal ions [7,8], nitro-aromatic explosive chemicals [9,10], pH [11], active substances [6,12–14] et al. However, most of the reported silicon quantum dots synthesized by hydrothermal method emit blue fluorescence [15–17], which limits their applications owing to the general blue auto-fluorescence of the food, environmental and biological substrate [18].

Nitrite (NO2 −) is a chemically active substance that is widely found in environments including food processing. NO2 − has antiseptic properties and can be used as a color retention agent to be added to meat products to maintain a good appearance. Besides, it prevents the production from Clostridium botulinum and enhances the safety of meat products [19]. However, excessive nitrites in food is harmful, especially for pregnant and infants [20]. Most of the nitrite in the human body comes from diet and water. The carcinogenic N-nitrosamines are produced by the interaction of NO2 − with amines that may cause esophageal cancer and gastric cancer [21]. Nitrite can react irreversibly with hemoglobin to produce methemoglobin, which reduces the ability to transport oxygen in the blood [22]. Therefore, to develop environment friendly and robust methods for routine detection of nitrite in food samples is of great importance.

Fluorescent nanomaterials including metal nanoclusters [23], semiconductor quantum dots [24], metal organic framework materials [25] and carbon dots [26] have been investigated in nitrite detection. Based on the combination of iodine and excess iodide leading to the etching of gold nanoclusters, Li et al. [27] developed a fluorescence quenching method to monitor NO2 − with a linear range from 50 μM to 5 mM and the LOD of 11.7 μM. Yu's group [28] found that the fluorescence of green emitting polyethylenimine-capped CdS quantum dots could be quenched by NO2 − and thus quantified NO2 − at concentrations of 1.0 × 10−7 -1.0 × 10−4 M with LOD 0.05 μM Tb3+ functionalized MOFs have been designed as a sensor for NO2 − [29]. The NO2 − has a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence of MOFs and this method could be employed effectively to analyze NO2 − in water samples. In Li's work [30], the nitrite sensor, blue fluorescent carbon nanodots, has a linear response at nitrite level of 20–500 μg/L. However, these methods have some limitations in practical routine applications. For examples, some anions interfered with the determination of nitrite when gold nanoclusters [27] and polyethylenimine-capped CdS quantum dots [28] were adopted. Besides, large scale use of metal-based fluorescent materials may cause unknown environmental effects. As an environment friendly material, fluorescent silicon quantum dots, to our best knowledge, has not been investigated in the detection of nitrite.

Herein, green-emitting silicon quantum dots (g-SiQDs) were synthesized by a one-pot facile hydrothermal procedure using N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (DAMO) and m-phenylenediamine (m-PD). The obtained g-SiQDs have longer emission wavelength than most of hydrothermally synthesized silicon quantum dots [15–17]. The fluorescence was selectively quenched by NO2 − through the synergy of IFE and PET effect. The analytical performance of g-SiQDs for NO2 − has been evaluated and proven to be suitable in routine analysis. Finally, various food samples such as bacon, ham and milk were tested with recoveries of 93.1–105.2%.

The reagents used were of analytical grade and can be used without purification. N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (DAMO) was purchased from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). M-phenylenediamine (m-PD), quinine sulfate, sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), copper sulfate (CuSO4), ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), aluminium chloride (AlCl3), cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), potassium bromide (KBr), mercury nitrate (HgNO3), cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O), sodium fluoride (NaF), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3), monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), sodium iodide (NaI), disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), citric acid, ascorbic acid (Vc), sucrose, lactose, glucose, tartaric acid, oxalic acid, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.,Ltd. (Shanghai, China). glutathione (GSH), l-cysteine (L-Cys), glutamate were acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water that had a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ cm.

Nitrite stock solution was prepared by dissolving 0.069 g sodium nitrite in 100 mL ultrapure water and stored in 4 °C. When used, it was diluted to the required concentration. HCl–KCl solutions (pH 1.0–2.0) were prepared from solutions contained 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M KCl with certain portions, and Na2HPO4-citrate buffer (pH 2.5–7.0) were prepared from solutions contained 0.1 M citric acid and 0.2 M Na2HPO4 with certain portions [31].

Size and morphology of the prepared g-SiQDs were measured using a high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) JEM-2100 TEM (JEOL Ltd., Japan). IS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA) was used to record Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Absorption spectra were measured on UV-2600 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The diffraction spectrum was obtained by Miniflex 600 powder X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime decay curves were obtained using a Felix 32 system (Photon Technology International, Birmingham, NJ, U.S.A.). Zeta potentials were tested by Zetasizer Nano ZEN3600 (Malvern, UK). The fluorescence analysis was conducted by RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) by setting the excitation and emission slits at 5 nm. A sonicator for 40 kHz (LEO-300S, Strider Instruments) was used for sonication.

The g-SiQDs were synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. Firstly, 10.0 mg of m-PD was dissolved in ultrapure water (10.0 mL) with the assistant of ultrasonic for 5 min, and DAMO (1.0 mL) was then added to the above solution. The mixture was moved to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave then reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. Next, the obtained solution was naturally cooled to room temperature. Finally, the solution was collected in a vacuum freeze dryer. 48 h later, the faint yellow powder g-SiQDs were gained and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The quantum yield (QY) of the g-SiQDs were measured using quinine sulfate as a reference standard (QY: 58% in 0.1 M H2SO4) [32]. To reduce the measurement error, the standard solution was freshly prepared [33]. QY of the g-SiQDs was calculated using the following equation (Eq. (A. 1)):
(1)
∅ =
∅ s
[
(
I
A s
η 2
)
(
I s
A
η s 2
)
]
where ∅ refers to the QY of g-SiQDs. I and A are integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance, respectively. Subscript s indicates the reference solution. η refers to the refractive index of the solvent. The absorbance value of the g-SiQDs and the reference solution were guaranteed to be 0.05 or less.

185 μL of g-SiQDs (0.1 mg/mL) and different volumes of nitrite solution were added to 2 mL of HCl–KCl solution (0.1 M, pH = 1.5), and diluted to 3 mL with the same solution. After reacting for 20 min at room temperature, RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to record the fluorescence of the mixture.

Food products including bacon, ham and milk were purchased from local supermarket. Sample preparation was performed according to the literature [34] but changed a little. For bacon and ham, 5 g of these two samples were cut as a slice and then ground in a glass tissue homogenizer, respectively. Then put into 40 mL of ultrapure water separately and sonic for 30 min to completely disperse the solid phase. The mixture was heated in a 75 °C water bath for 30 min. After cooled to room temperature, the homogenate was filtered through a filter paper and a 0.22 μm micro-porous membrane to remove residues and insoluble substance, followed by fluorescence analysis. For milk sample, the preparation was the same as above except not cut into slices and tissue homogenizer was not being used.
The standard addition method was used to evaluate the accuracy of the method. The detection procedure was the same as above, except that the solution contained 200 μL of the food sample solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the g-SiQDs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that the photoluminescence spectrum of carbon dots is red-shifted after being functionalized with amino group [35]. Jin's group found that when graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is functionalized with an amino group, the band gap decreases and the photoluminescence spectrum undergoes a red shift. The change of band gap is due to the charge transfer between the functional group and the GQDs [36]. Compared with alkyl groups, amine-terminated silicon quantum dots are more effective in showing strong fluorescence [37]. Inspired by these works, we successfully synthesized silicon quantum dots by a one-pot hydrothermal method using amino-rich DAMO and m-PD. A proposed synthesis mechanism is that, DAMO is first hydrolyzed to N-[3-(trihydroxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine in aqueous phase. Afterwards, N-[3-(trihydroxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine molecules are reduced by m-phenylenediamine under the high temperature and high pressure condition (180 °C, 6 h). Finally the silicon quantum dots having more amino group on the surface are formed by the combination of reduced N-[3-(trihydroxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine. To understand the role of m-PD, we synthesized silicon quantum dots without adding m-PD, and other conditions remain unchanged [38]. The fluorescence of silicon quantum dots are very weak and in the blue region in the absence of m-PD. The presence of m-PD makes the surface of silicon quantum dots contain more amino groups, and its fluorescence is in the green region. The obtained silicon quantum dots emitted green fluorescence, larger than most reported silicon quantum dots synthesized by the same methods. Thus, an environmental friendly green-emitting fluorescent silicon quantum dots were obtained. Then, the characterization of the obtained g-SiQDs was performed in detail.

As can be seen from the TEM images (Fig. 1 a), the g-SiQDs represent a spherical shape. It can be observed that some of silicon quantum dots are crystalline. In these crystals, the lattice plane spacing is 0.31 nm (HRTEM image, inset in Fig. 1a). These results indicated the g-SiQDs were successfully synthesized. The size distribution histograms of g-SiQDs calculated with more than 170 particles from TEM image is displayed in Fig. 1b, and the average diameter is about 3.5 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern shows a broad peak (Fig. 1c), which is consistent with report [39,40]. The appearance of broad peak is due to the small size and mostly amorphous nature of the g-SiQDs [41]. To identify the surface structure of the g-SiQDs, the FT-IR spectrum was measured (Fig. 1d). The absorbance peaks at 1597 cm−1 and 3421 cm−1 are attributed to the N–H bending vibration and stretching vibration, and peaks at 781 cm−1 and 697 cm−1 are belonged to N–H wagging vibration and secondary amine vibration [39,42,43], respectively. A strong peak involves Si–O stretching occurs at 910 cm−1 in the Si–O–H group. The signal at 1131 and 1040 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of Si–O bonding [44]. The 1466 cm−1 absorptions are pertained to C=C vibration of the benzene ring [45]. In addition, the C–N stretching vibrations shows at 1328 cm−1, while C–O stretching vibrations at 1211 cm−1 [46]. We performed FT-IR characterization of the raw material m-PD (Fig. 1d). The peaks at 1601 cm−1 and 1496 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of m-PD still exist in g-SiQDs (1597 cm−1 and 1466 cm−1) clearly, indicating that the N–H bending vibration and CC vibration of the benzene ring are derived from m-PD. These results indicate the existence of m-PD structure on g-SiQDs.

For the purpose of acquiring more convincible evidence about surface groups and the elemental composition of the g-SiQDs, we performed XPS characterization. The full-scan analysis is showed in Fig. 2 a and it presents five major peaks at 102.2, 284.8, 398.9, and 531.8 eV, which are corresponded to Si 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s [40]. The high resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2b) indicates the presence of C–Si (284.4 eV), C–C/CC (284.9 eV), C–N (285.7 eV), C–O (286.7 eV), respectively [40]. The contribution at 398.7, 399.3, and 400.4 eV are assigned to N–Si, C–N–C, and N–H in the high resolution spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2c), respectively [2]. In addition, two peaks appear at 530.7 eV and 531.7 eV are assigned to Si–O in the spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 2d), while the peak 532.2 eV is signified to C–O [9]. The Si 2p spectrum can be decomposed in three contribution at 101.7 eV, 102.2 eV, and 102.8 eV, which are corresponded to Si–C, Si–N, and Si–O (Fig. 2e) [47], respectively. We synthesized g-SiQDs with N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (DAMO) as the silicon source, so it contains C–O bonds. The Si–N bond may originate in the process of hydrothermal synthesis of g-SiQDs.

Optical properties of the g-SiQDs were evaluated by fluorescence spectrum and UV–vis absorption. The absorption spectrum of g-SiQDs is displayed in Fig. 3 a. Considering the g-SiQDs is composed of SiQD core and surface groups, and the absorption of g-SiQDs should come from both of them. Thus, the significant absorption peaks at 258 nm can be probably assigned to π-π* transition of aromatic groups on the surface [48], and n-π* transition appears at 394 nm [19], while the shoulder peak at about 290 nm may be due to Γ-Γ direct band gap transition of SiQD core [38]. The fluorescence spectrum indicates that 503 nm is the maximum emission wavelength of the g-SiQDs (green line in Fig. 3a, excited at 387 nm). With the increase of excitation wavelength from 360 to 410 nm (measured every 5 nm), the fluorescence emission peak at 503 nm does not shift (Fig. 3b). The QY of g-SiQDs had been measured and calculated to be 18.5%.

The pH-stability, photo-stability and ionic strength stability were utilized to evaluate the stability of the synthesized g-SiQDs. In the range of pH 9 to 12, the fluorescence intensity is relatively weak, while it is strong and remains relatively constant from pH 1 to 5 (Fig. 4 a). At pH 6 to 8, the amino groups on the surface of g-SiQDs change from protonation to deprotonation, which affects the surface charge of g-SiQDs, resulting in changes of fluorescence intensity [9]. Photo-stability of g-SiQDs can be seen from Fig. 4b, and no significant change of g-SiQDs fluorescence is found after 12 h of illuminating. Then, the result of temperature stability (Fig. 4c) shows that the fluorescence is negligible changed below 45 °C. Finally, we tested the salt tolerance. We measured the fluorescence intensity of g-SiQDs that was incubated with various concentrations of NaCl from 0 to 1000 mM (Fig. 4d). The fluorescence intensity changes are negligible. In summary, the excellent stability made the as-prepared g-SiQDs appropriate for many practical applications.

For the purpose of obtaining the highest sensitivity of g-SiQDs to NO2 −, we investigated the detection conditions, including the pH of system, response time and reaction temperature. The pH of the reaction system is important because the pH affects both the fluorescence properties of g-SiQDs and the reaction process. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of g-SiQDs is different with variation of pH, and reaches the maximum extent at pH 1.0 and 1.5 (Fig. 5b, F0 and F are represent the fluorescence intensity of g-SiQDs in the absence and presence of NO2 −, respectively). The obtain g-SiQDs show strong fluorescence under acidic conditions, while the fluorescence has different degrees of quenching from neutral to alkaline (Fig. 4a). Considering the weaker original fluorescence of g-SiQDs in weak-acidic and alkaline conditions, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of nitrite towards g-SiQDs will not be very high when pH was higher than 3.0, and it is reasonable to achieve high quenching efficiency under acidic conditions. Therefore, we chose pH 1.5 as the optimal pH condition. Reaction time is another crucial factor affecting the yield of the reaction. Fig. 5c shows the change of quenching efficiency at a 20 μM NO2 − during 35 min, and the quenching efficiency reaches maximum and keep almost constant after 20 min. As a result, 20 min was selected as the optimal reaction time in the further experiments. The same strategy had been taken to gain the optimal reaction temperature. According to Fig. 5d, the following experiments were carried out at room temperature.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescence responses of the g-SiQDs to different concentrations of NO2 − were examined to reveal the sensitivity of the sensor. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, fluorescence intensity of g-SiQDs decreases with increasing of NO2 − concentration. A good linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency and concentration of NO2 − is displayed in Fig. 6b with the NO2 − concentration ranging from 0.1 to 6.0 μM. The linear regression equation is (F0–F)/F0 = 0.0135x-0.0048 with R2 = 0.9969. The detection limit is 25 nM (based on a signal-to-noise ratio S/N = 3). Compared with other methods (Table 1 ), the proposed method based on g-SiQDs fluorescence sensing has lower detection limit and a wider linear range.

To confirm the quenching mechanism between NO2 − and g-SiQDs, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of NO2 − was investigated. It can be seen that an overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NO2 − and the excitation spectrum of g-SiQDs (Fig. 7 a), which is consistent with the inner filter effect (IFE) reported in the literature and indicates that inner filter effect (IFE) probably occurred in the quenching mechanism [55]. In addition, the FT-IR spectrum confirms that surface groups of the g-SiQDs have no change (Fig. 7b), so as the diameter (Figs. 1b and 7c). Zeta potential was carried out to further discuss the mechanism. The zeta potential of the g-SiQDs decreases from 9.35 to 6.90 mV upon addition of NO2 − (Fig. 7d). The possible quenching mechanism is explained to photo induced electron transfer (PET) effect. The nitrite group is strongly electron withdrawing, electron transfer from the LUMO of excited g-SiQDs to the LUMO of NO2 −. Then, the excited electrons of g-SiQDs are unable to transit back and emit photons.

Previous reports have demonstrated that, if the quenching type is dynamic quenching effect (DQE) or static quenching effect (SQE), the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (A. 2)) would be linear [56]:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
Q
where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of the NO2 −, respectively. KSV and [Q] are the Stern-Volmer constant and the NO2 − concentration, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7e, there is an upward curve between F0/F and the concentration of NO2 −, indicating that the quenching mechanism can't be simply verified as dynamic or static quenching effect. Although the change of lifetime can be ignored (<0.2 ns, Fig. 7f) before and after addition the quencher NO2 −, the lifetime of g-SiQDs added NO2 − can be provided by un-reacted g-SiQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the proposed method for NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the sensor has been investigated. The fluorescence intensity of g-SiQDs was measured in the presence of some cations (Na+, K+, Ca2 +, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Cd2+ and NH4 +), anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CH3COO−, HCO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, SO4 2−) and common substances in biological or foods, including citric acid, ascorbic acid, sucrose, lactose, glucose, tartaric acid, oxalic acid, GSH, L-Cys, hydrogen peroxide, and glutamate. The concentration of NO2 − was 5 μM, cations, anions, common substances in biological or foods were 100 folds (500 μM) that of NO2 −, while the concentration of Fe3+ was 20 folds (100 μM) that of the target analyte. When adding 500 μM Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of g-SiQDs was quenched to a greater extent (about 34%). For the purpose of making the degree of quenching less than 5%, we reduced the concentration of Fe3+. When add 100 μM Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity quenched by 2%. As far as we know, the level of Fe3+ in biological or foods is less than 100 μM. This result can indicate that the method shows good specificity for NO2 −. As shown in Fig. 8 , only NO2 − caused overt decrease of fluorescence intensity and actually, and no obvious fluorescence changes could be observed in the presence of other substance. As analytes, glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys) and glutamate contain amino groups, which are positively charged and cannot interact with positively charged g-SiQDS. Then, from the UV–Vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 8d, only the absorption wavelength of Fe3+ can overlap with the excitation spectrum of g-SiQDS. When the concentration of added Fe3+ is 100 μM, the IFE is even smaller. This result is consistent with the results of the selectivity experiment. This result demonstrated that the proposed method possessed high specificity toward NO2 − in food samples. Then it can be utilized in NO2-detection in food samples.

The practical application of this method was then investigated using three different kinds of foods including bacon, ham and milk. As revealed in Table 2 , the relative standard deviations (RSD) and the recoveries are in the range of 0.9–5.3% and 93.1–105.2%, respectively. Therefore, the results indicate that the proposed method were reliable and meet the analytical requirements.

In summary, an environmental friendly green emitting silicon quantum dots were prepared by simple one-pot hydrothermal approach without further modification. The synthesized g-SiQDs have maximum emission wavelength at 503 nm, and exhibit excellent water solubility, ionic strength stability and photo-stability. It was found that the green fluorescence of g-SiQDs can be quenched by NO2 − due to the synergistic of IFE and PET effect. Significantly, this method shows good specificity for NO2 − over many co-existences with LOD of 25 nM, and has been utilized for the detection of NO2 − in food samples successfully. The g-SiQDs used in this method are simple to prepare and no complicated sample pre-treatment is required during the detection process, which is very suitable for rapid routine detection of NO2 in food.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Na Wei: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, preparation, Reviewing and Editing. Meng-Xia Wei: Infrared spectroscopy characterization. Huang Bo-Hui: Zeta potential measurement. Xiao-Feng Guo: Conceptualization, Resources, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Reviewing and Editing, Funding acquisition. Hong Wang: Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31670370 and 21777126, Beijing, China).
